title: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder
authors: Nora D. Volkow, Aidan Hampson, Ruben Baler

items.title: Prevalence and Demographics of Cannabis Use
items.content: The widespread use of cannabis is highlighted by recent surveys, showing significant consumption across various age groups. The data reflects both the prevalence and demographic distribution of users.
keyPoints: Cannabis/marijuana is used by more than 150 million people worldwide.
In 2023, 42 million U.S. adults reported using marijuana in the last month.
10 million individuals aged 12-25 used marijuana monthly in 2023.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.title: Legal Definitions and Regulatory Changes of Cannabis Products
items.content: The legal status and definitions of cannabis products have evolved, distinguishing between marijuana and hemp based on D9THC content. This distinction affects both regulatory frameworks and consumer perceptions.
keyPoints: Marijuana is defined as containing >0.3% D9THC by dry weight.
Hemp contains no more than 0.3% D9THC by dry weight.
24 states, 3 U.S. territories, and Washington, DC have legalized nonmedicinal cannabis use.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.title: Risks Associated with Different Methods of Cannabis Consumption
items.content: The method of cannabis consumption significantly influences the risk profile, with inhalation methods like vaping presenting higher addiction risks due to rapid absorption. Edibles, while slower in onset, pose a greater risk for accidental overdosing.
keyPoints: Inhalation delivers high D9THC doses rapidly, increasing addiction risk.
Edible products have slower onset and are favored by younger users.
Edibles are associated with higher accidental dosing probability.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.title: Mechanism of Euphoria from D9THC
items.content: D9THC induces euphoria by acting as a partial agonist on CB1 receptors, which are located on excitatory glutamatergic and inhibitory GABAergic interneurons. This action modulates synaptic signaling in brain regions associated with stress, mood, and reward.
keyPoints: D9THC is a partial agonist of cannabinoid receptors (CB1R and CB2R).
Euphorigenic effects are mediated through CB1Rs on glutamatergic and GABAergic interneurons.
CB1Rs are located in brain regions processing stress, mood, and reward.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.title: Anxiolytic Effects of D9THC via Amygdala Modulation
items.content: D9THC's anxiolytic effects are mediated by its action on the amygdala, a brain region crucial for threat perception and emotional reactivity.
keyPoints: The anxiolytic effects of D9THC involve modulation of the amygdala.
Amygdala is critical for threat perception and emotional reactivity.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.title: Understanding D9THC Bioavailability by Route of Administration
items.content: D9THC bioavailability varies significantly depending on the route of administration. Smoking offers a higher bioavailability (10–35%) compared to oral ingestion, which is much lower (5-6%). This difference is due to first-pass metabolism in the liver and intestine for orally consumed products.
keyPoints: Smoked D9THC exhibits 10–35% bioavailability.
Oral consumption has a bioavailability of 5–6%.
First-pass metabolism significantly reduces oral bioavailability.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.title: Pharmacokinetics and Detection Window of D9THC
items.content: D9THC reaches maximal plasma concentration within 5–10 minutes after smoking, with subjective effects peaking at 20–30 minutes. The terminal elimination half-life of its metabolite, 11-norcarboxy-THC, ranges from 20 to over 35 hours, allowing detection in urine for days or weeks depending on usage frequency.
keyPoints: Maximal plasma D9THC concentration occurs within 5–10 minutes post-smoking.
Subjective effects peak at 20–30 minutes after smoking.
11-norcarboxy-THC elimination half-life is 20 to >35 hours.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.title: Impact of Fatty Foods on Oral D9THC Bioavailability
items.content: Consuming cannabis orally with fatty foods can significantly increase D9THC bioavailability by 200–400% due to enhanced bile release, which emulsifies cannabinoids and facilitates their absorption. This increased bioavailability is a result of bypassing hepatic elimination through lymphatic transport.
keyPoints: Fatty foods can increase oral D9THC bioavailability by 200–400%.
Bile release from fatty foods enhances cannabinoid emulsification.
Lymphatic transport increases bioavailability but delays onset.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.title: Cannabis Use and Fetal Development Risks
items.content: Cannabis exposure during pregnancy may lead to fetal growth disturbances, lower birth weight, preterm delivery, increased intracranial volumes, and altered brain development. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists advises against cannabis use in pregnant or planning women.
keyPoints: Potential negative effects on the brain during early life stages
Association between intrauterine exposure to D9THC and lifelong health disturbances
Evidence suggests lower birth weight, fetal growth issues, preterm delivery
ABCD study indicates increased intracranial volumes and impacts on visuospatial processing
Significant effects on white matter integrity in childhood, especially the fornix
American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends discouraging marijuana use during pregnancy
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.title: Cannabis Use and Adolescent Brain Development
items.content: Adolescent cannabis use is linked to structural and functional brain changes, potentially affecting cognitive functions such as memory. The ABCD study suggests that early exposure may lead to lower episodic memory scores.
keyPoints: Increased vulnerability of children and adolescents to harmful effects
Association with lower grades, IQ, higher dropout risk
Brain imaging studies show reduced brain connectivity and cortical thickness
Negative dose-dependent correlation between cannabis use at age 14 and prefrontal cortex thickness at age 19
ABCD study findings: early exposure linked to poorer performance on memory tasks
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.title: Risk Factors for Cannabis Use Disorder (CUD)
items.content: Repeated cannabis use, particularly during adolescence, increases the risk of developing CUD. Key factors include early age of initiation, frequent use, and exposure to high THC content cannabis.
keyPoints: Cannabis use disorder is defined by DSM-5 as a problematic pattern leading to significant impairment or distress.
Risk factors: earlier age of initiation, frequency of use, high THC content.
Receptor density in the brain recovers within 28 days of abstinence.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.title: Effective Prevention Strategies for Adolescent Cannabis Use
items.content: Preventing cannabis use during adolescence reduces the risk of CUD and other substance disorders. Evidence-based strategies include school-based programs that combine antidrug information with skills training.
keyPoints: Prevention reduces risk for CUD and other substance use disorders.

Effective interventions: Keepin' It Real, Life Skills, InShape (individual), Brief Strategic Family Therapy, CPP, Familia Adelante (family), The Abecedarian Project, Midwestern Prevention Project, Caring School Community (community).
School-based programs are most widely implemented.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.question: According to the 2023 United States National Survey on Drug Use and Health, how many people aged 12-25 years used marijuana each month?
items.explanation: The survey estimated that 10 million younger people (12-25 year olds) consumed marijuana each month.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.question: What is the legal definition of 'hemp' in terms of D9THC content by dry weight?
items.explanation: 'Hemp' is defined as cannabis plant material, extracts, and derivatives that contain no more than 0.3% D9THC by dry weight.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.question: Which cannabinoid receptor does D9THC primarily act on to mediate its effects?
items.explanation: D9THC acts on neuronal type 1 cannabinoid receptors (CB1R) to mediate its euphoric and medicinal properties.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.question: What is the perceived effect of D8THC compared to D9THC according to users?
items.explanation: Users report that D8THC is milder than D9THC and perceive it to be legal.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.question: Which receptors mediate the euphorigenic effects of D9THC?
items.explanation: The euphorigenic effects of D9THC are mediated through CB1Rs.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.question: What is the primary role of endocannabinoids in synaptic signaling?
items.explanation: Endocannabinoids modulate synaptic signaling by inhibiting overstimulated synapses.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.question: Where are the rewarding effects of D9THC thought to be mediated?
items.explanation: The rewarding effects of D9THC are mediated by modulating activity in the midbrain ventral tegmental area (VTA).
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.question: What is the bioavailability range of D9THC when smoked?
items.explanation: The bioavailability of smoked D9THC ranges from 10–35%, depending on individual lung capacity.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.question: Which phase in the pharmacokinetics of smoked cannabis is characterized by an alpha half-life (~6 min) and involves absorption into lipophilic tissues?
items.explanation: The initial distribution phase has an alpha half-life of ~6 minutes, governed by absorption into lipophilic tissues.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.question: What is the primary metabolite detected during marijuana urinalysis?
items.explanation: The primary metabolite detected during marijuana urinalysis is the long-lasting, but pharmacologically inactive, 11-norcarboxy THC (11-COOH-THC).
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.question: How does consuming cannabis with fatty foods affect its bioavailability?
items.explanation: Consuming cannabis with fatty foods can increase its bioavailability by 200–400% because fatty foods stimulate bile release, aiding absorption.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.question: Which of the following is a potential negative effect of cannabis exposure during early fetal development?
items.explanation: Exposure to D9THC can lead to cognitive and memory impairments due to perturbation of ECS signaling during fetal development.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.question: What did the Adolescent Brain and Child Development (ABCD) study suggest about prenatal cannabis exposure?
options: It has no effect on brain development.
It increases intracranial volumes and affects visuospatial processing.
items.explanation: The ABCD study provides evidence that prenatal cannabis exposure can increase intracranial volumes and blunt the development of visuospatial processing.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.question: What is associated with a dose-dependent negative correlation in adolescents according to European research?
options: Cannabis use at age 14 and increased prefrontal cortex thickness at age 19
Cannabis use at age 14 and decreased prefrontal cortex thickness at age 19
Cannabis use at age 10 and increased hippocampal volume at age 15
Cannabis use at age 10 and decreased hippocampal volume at age 15
items.explanation: Research found a negative, dose-dependent correlation between cannabis use at age 14 and prefrontal cortex thickness at age 19.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.question: Which of the following factors increases the risk of developing cannabis use disorder (CUD)?
options: Earlier age of initiation, frequency of use, and exposure to high THC content
Late age of initiation, infrequent use, and low THC content
Only genetic predisposition without any environmental influence
Use of cannabis in adulthood with no prior history
items.explanation: The risk of CUD increases with earlier age of initiation, frequency of use, and exposure to cannabis with high THC content.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.question: What is the peak time frame for withdrawal symptoms after discontinuation of regular cannabis use?
items.explanation: Withdrawal syndrome peaks within 1–3 days of drug discontinuation.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.question: Which type of prevention interventions are most widely implemented for reducing cannabis use during adolescence?
items.explanation: School-based prevention programs are the most widely implemented.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.statement: Marijuana remains federally illegal as of May 2024.
items.explanation: As stated, marijuana is still federally illegal as of May 2024.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.statement: Edible cannabis products are more bioavailable than inhaled D9THC.
items.explanation: Edible D9THC-infused products are less bioavailable than inhaled D9THC.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.statement: High doses of D9THC can induce anxiety, paranoia, and panic.
items.explanation: Consumption of high doses of D9THC is known to induce anxiety, paranoia, and panic.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.statement: The anxiolytic effects of D9THC are mediated by its action on the hippocampus.
items.explanation: The anxiolytic effects of D9THC are mediated by its effects on the amygdala, not the hippocampus.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.statement: The subjective effects of smoked D9THC are maximal within the first 5-10 minutes.
items.explanation: Subjective effects tend to be maximal at 20–30 min after smoking, not within the first 5-10 minutes.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.statement: Oral consumption of cannabis results in a hysteresis effect similar to inhalation.
items.explanation: No hysteresis is observed with oral consumption, unlike after inhalation.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.statement: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommends discouraging marijuana use by women who are pregnant or planning a pregnancy.
items.explanation: They recommend against the use due to potential harmful effects on fetal development.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.statement: EVALI is associated with vitamin E acetate used in FDA-approved marijuana e-liquids.
items.explanation: EVALI was linked to black-market marijuana e-liquids containing vitamin E acetate, an unapproved agent.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · HARMFUL EFFECTS

items.statement: The FDA has approved specific medications for the treatment of cannabis use disorder.
items.explanation: There are currently no U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved medications for CUD.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.statement: Insomnia is a withdrawal symptom that often persists longer than other symptoms.
items.explanation: Insomnia often persists longer and may contribute to relapse.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS USE DISORDER | ☐ PREVENTION | TREATMENT

items.assertion: The demand for high D9THC-containing cannabis strains has increased.
items.reason: Marijuana legalization has increased the supply of such strains.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.reason: Pharmacologic studies support lower potency of D8THC than D9THC.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why D8THC is considered non-intoxicating.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder

items.assertion: Exogenous cannabinoids disrupt important ECS neuroregulatory processes.
items.reason: The effect of intoxicating cannabinoids is defined by dose and their period of effect, not subtle 'just in time' synaptic synthesis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why exogenous cannabinoids disrupt ECS processes.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · PHARMACOLOGIC EFFECTS

items.assertion: Regular use of D9THC results in tolerance to its pharmacologic effects.
items.reason: A given D9THC plasma concentration may not correlate with similar impairment levels in occasional versus regular users.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why regular use results in tolerance.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS

items.assertion: The pharmacokinetics of smoked cannabis flower and commercial e-liquids have been directly compared.
items.reason: No data are available to compare the pharmacokinetics of smoked flower and commercial e-liquids or solid concentrates.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as no such comparative data exist.
items.reference: Cannabis and Cannabis Use Disorder · CANNABIS PHARMACOKINETICS
